detailed_description.heading: Gigabit Passive Ethernet Repeater
detailed_description.text: The MikroTik GPeR (Gigabit Passive Ethernet Repeater) is a key element of the GPEN (Gigabit Passive Ethernet Network) concept, designed to replace traditional GPON installations with a more affordable and easier-to-deploy Ethernet-based alternative. It allows network operators, installers, and building owners to build long-distance Ethernet networks using standard CAT6 cabling, without the complexity and cost of fiber infrastructure.

detailed_description.heading: Extended Ethernet Range
detailed_description.text: The GPeR makes it possible to extend an Ethernet link far beyond the usual 100-meter limitation. Each additional GPeR unit adds another hop, enabling distances of up to 100–150 meters to standard network devices, or up to 210 meters between two GPeR units (depending on cable quality). By chaining multiple repeaters, Ethernet links can be extended up to 1.5 km when one side provides PoE power, or up to 3 km when both ends of the link have PoE sources. This makes the GPeR especially useful in high-rise buildings, large residential complexes, and multi-section properties where very long cable runs are unavoidable.

detailed_description.heading: Passive PoE Passthrough
detailed_description.text: The device supports Passive PoE passthrough, allowing power to be delivered together with data over the same Ethernet cable. If the connected device does not support PoE-in, the passthrough function can be easily disabled by removing the onboard jumpers, ensuring safe operation with non-PoE equipment.

detailed_description.heading: Typical Use Cases
detailed_description.text: Typical use cases include extending Ethernet links to remote floors or rooftops, connecting access points and switches in large apartment buildings, or building centralized GPEN-based networks where power and connectivity are distributed efficiently across long distances. Compact, passive, and reliable, MikroTik GPeR is a practical solution for scalable Ethernet networks where distance, simplicity, and cost-efficiency matter.

detailed_description.heading: Deployment Note
detailed_description.text: * When planning deployments, voltage drop over long cable runs and multiple GPeR units should be taken into account; if power becomes insufficient, an alternative power source can be used.

items.name: Power Method
items.value: 24V Passive PoE, 48V Passive PoE, 802.3af PoE, 802.3at PoE+

comments.name: Steve
comments.text:
Very very clever little device.
Technically all it is (from my understanding) is a 2 port ethernet switch powered by passive POE.
1-IN and 1-OUT and boosts the levels of the DATA lines back to spec.
Ethernet being bi-directionnal, there is no point in putting this device at one end or the other of a long cable run, it needs to be mid-way of the cable run to be efficient.
